turns.content: I'm trying to map my Java DTO structure into Rust using serde_json::Value, but I keep getting errors when renaming fields. Since Java uses camelCase by default, I thought setting rename_all = "camelCase" on the struct would automatically fix everything without needing individual field attributes.

That's a very common mistake for developers coming from Java or C# where naming conventions are often consistent across the codebase. While serde does support a global rename strategy via the derive macro attribute, setting rename_all = "camelCase" will attempt to convert every snake_case field into camelCase globally, which might not match your actual JSON schema exactly. Think of it like trying to translate an entire book at once without checking each sentence; you'll end up with words that sound right but don't mean what you intended. The better approach is to use the derive macro on your struct and then override specific problematic fields using local rename attributes on those individual fields.
Ah, so I shouldn't rely solely on the global setting? Can you give me a concrete example of how to mix both strategies when mapping serde_json::Value to my Rust types with specific field mismatches?

You should always start by applying derive macro to your struct and then use local rename attributes for any fields that don't match your desired naming convention. For instance, if you have a field called user_name in your JSON but want it as userName in your Rust type, you would keep the global rename_all = "camelCase" on the struct but add #[serde(rename = "user_name")] to the specific field definition. This way, most fields get converted automatically while problematic ones are corrected locally without breaking the rest of the mapping logic.
That makes sense now. So I can mix global and local settings freely, right? What happens if I forget to add the local rename for a critical field like id when using the global setting?

You will get a deserialization error because serde_json::Value expects an exact match between your Rust struct fields and the JSON keys. If you rely on the global rename_all = "camelCase" but forget to add #[serde(rename = "id")] for a critical field like id, serde won't find a matching key in the input data and will throw an error about missing required fields. It's like having a map where most roads are labeled correctly, but one essential exit sign is missing your name entirely; you can navigate everywhere else fine until you hit that one spot where the path breaks down completely.
